coredata.dc:title: The selectivity of the carboxylate groups terminated carbon dots switched by buffer solutions for the detection of multi-metal ions
coredata.dc:creator: Li, Chun
Liu, Weijian
Ren, Yanjing
Sun, Xiaobo
Pan, Wei
Wang, Jinping
coredata.dc:description: In this work, the carboxylate groups terminated carbon dots (−OOC-C-dots) were synthesized by the thermal pyrolysis of citric acid at 180°C for 40h under air. The −OOC-C-dots with excellent water solubility showed bright blue emission under excitation at 360nm. The blue emission fluorescence of the −OOC-C-dots can be quenched by Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, and Fe3+ ions in HNO3 solution at pH=5.0. Most interestingly, the −OOC-C-dots exhibited highly selective response to Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer solution, to Pb2+ in Britton-Robinson buffer solution, and to Hg2+ in PBS buffer solution at pH=5.0, respectively. Moreover, good linear relationships were found between (F0-F)/F0 and the concentrations of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in the corresponding buffer solutions. As a result, the −OOC-C-dots can be explored as a chemosensor for the label-free detection of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ with selectivity being switched by different buffer systems. By the proposed method, the selective detection of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in real water samples (mineral and tap), even in the presence of high concentration of interfering ions, was accurately achieved. Hence, a new method for the potential detection of multi-metal ions can be developed by using one carbon-dot-based chemosensor with switchable selectivity in different buffer solutions.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Detection
Metal ions
Optical sensors

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of multi-metal ions in real samples
The −OOC-C-dots based fluorescent chemosensor for the detection of multi-metal ions

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: A) XRD pattern and B) FTIR spectrum of the −OOC-C-dots.

Fluorescence spectra of the −OOC-C-dots in HNO3 solution at pH=5.0 (0.2mgml−1) with increasing excitation from 300 to 500nm with 20nm increments. Inset: normalized fluorescence spectra at different excitation wavelength.
The effect of A) the solution pH and B) the NaCl concentration (0, 25, 50, 100, 200, 400mM) on the fluorescence of the −OOC-C-dots (0.2mgml−1, pH=5.0).

Normalized fluorescence intensity at λex =360nm of the −OOC-C-dots solution (0.2mgml−1) in the presence of various metal ions in A) HNO3 solution, pH=5.0; B) NaAc-HAc buffer solution, 0.1M, pH=5.0; C) Britton-Robinson buffer solution, 0.04M, pH=5.0; D) PBS buffer solution, 0.1M, pH=5.0.

Fluorescence emission spectra of the −OOC-C-dots solution (0.2mgml−1) in A) NaAc-HAc buffer solution, 0.1M, pH=5.0; C) Britton-Robinson buffer solution, 0.04M, pH=5.0; E) PBS buffer solution, 0.1M, pH=5.0 at various concentrations of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ (from top to bottom: 0–800μM), excitation at 360nm. And the linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and the concentrations of B) Fe3+, D) Pb2+ and F) Hg2+.
Results for the detection of multi-metal ions in spiked mineral water and tap water samples.

Results for the detection of multi-metal ions in the mineral water and tap water samples spiked with interfering ions including Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, and Fe3+. The total concentration of the interfering ions is 210μM in the samples for the detection of Fe3+ and 350μM for the detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+.

head.ce:title: The selectivity of the carboxylate groups terminated carbon dots switched by buffer solutions for the detection of multi-metal ions

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences, Qingdao Agricultural University, Qingdao 266109, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemical and Pharmaceutical Sciences

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this work, the carboxylate groups terminated carbon dots (−OOC-C-dots) were synthesized by the thermal pyrolysis of citric acid at 180°C for 40h under air. The −OOC-C-dots with excellent water solubility showed bright blue emission under excitation at 360nm. The blue emission fluorescence of the −OOC-C-dots can be quenched by Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, and Fe3+ ions in HNO3 solution at pH=5.0. Most interestingly, the −OOC-C-dots exhibited highly selective response to Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer solution, to Pb2+ in Britton-Robinson buffer solution, and to Hg2+ in PBS buffer solution at pH=5.0, respectively. Moreover, good linear relationships were found between (F0-F)/F0 and the concentrations of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in the corresponding buffer solutions. As a result, the −OOC-C-dots can be explored as a chemosensor for the label-free detection of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ with selectivity being switched by different buffer systems. By the proposed method, the selective detection of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in real water samples (mineral and tap), even in the presence of high concentration of interfering ions, was accurately achieved. Hence, a new method for the potential detection of multi-metal ions can be developed by using one carbon-dot-based chemosensor with switchable selectivity in different buffer solutions.

ce:section.ce:para: Iron is the most essential trace metal element in all living organisms. Its deficiency or overloading can disturb cellular homeostasis and metabolism, which may cause cellular damages and further result in many different diseases, such as hepatitis, cancer, neurodegeneration Alzheimer’s disease, and Parkinson’s disease [1–4]. Mercury and lead as highly toxic heavy metal ions are widespread in the environment such as in air, water, soil, and food. They can easily exert adverse effects on human health even at low concentration [5,6]. Therefore, it is particularly important to develop methods with high sensitivity and selectivity for the detection of such metal ions in both environmental and biological samples [7]. Several techniques for the analysis of transition metal ions in various samples have been developed, such as atomic absorption spectrometry (AAS) [8], inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES) [9], inductively coupled plasma mass spectroscopy (ICP-MS) [10], and fluorescent chemosensors [11]. Fluorescent chemosensors have become more attractive tools for the detection of metal ions owing to their high sensitivity, selectivity, and capacity for rapid, real-time monitoring [12–15]. Most widely used fluorescent chemosensors include fluorescent metal nanoclusters [16], organic dyes [17], semiconductor quantum dots (QD) [18], fluorescent metal organic frameworks [19]. However, practical applications of these fluorescent chemosensors may be restricted by their cytotoxicity, environmental unfriendly, high costs, and complex equipment and treatment processes.

Recently, carbon dots, as excellent chemosensors, have been a focus in the field of analytical chemistry due to their high performances, including bright fluorescence [20], excellent photostability [21], tunable fluorescence emission [22], favorable biocompatibility [23], low toxicity [24] and good water solubility [25]. For example, a variety of methods based on carbon dots have been reported for chemically sensing Hg2+ [26,27], Cu2+ [28,29], Fe3+ [30,31], Pb2+ [32], Cr(VI) [33], and Ag+ [34]. For most of these methods, one carbon dot-based chemosensor can only selectively detect one type of metal ion, although a few studies had reported that it could be applied for the selective detection of two types of metals ions. For example, Zhu et al. [35] found that fluorescent carbon dots could serve as a chemosensor for selective detection of two types of metal ions, Fe3+ and Ag+. Karfa et al. [36] presented that the carbon dots synthesized by reflux of cysteine in concentrated H2SO4 could be used to detect Fe3+ and Cd2+. Also, Zhang’s group [37] employed the graphitic carbon dots prepared by hydrothermal treatment of bulk g-C3N4 to detect Fe3+ and Cu2+ in aqueous solution.

To our knowledge, few published reports are available that one carbon-dot-based chemosensor was used to selectively detect three types of metal ions. In this work, the carboxylate groups terminated carbon dots (−OOC-C-dots) were synthesized by a facile method (i.e. thermal pyrolysis of citric acid at 180°C for 40h under air). The as-synthesized −OOC-C-dots were quenched by Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+ and Fe3+ ions in HNO3 solution at pH=5.0. However, the selectivity of −OOC-C-dots can be switched to Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer solution, to Pb2+ in Britton-Robinson buffer solution, and to Hg2+ in PBS buffer solution. The −OOC-C-dot- based chemosensor was successfully applied to the practical detection of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ ions in mineral water and tap water samples. Therefore, a new method for the potential detection of multi-metal ions using one carbon-dot-based chemosensor with switchable selectivity in different buffer solutions can be developed.

AgNO3, CaCl2, NiCl2, CoCl2, CuCl2, Fe(NO3)3, Hg(Ac)2, MgCl2, MnSO4, Pb(NO3)2, AlCl3, BaCl2, CrCl3, NaCl, LiNO3, NH4HCO3, KCl, and Zn(NO3)2 were purchased from Damao Chemical Corp (Tianjin, China). Citric acid, Acetic Acid, Sodium acetate trihydrate, Ethylene diamine tetraacetic acid (EDTA), NaH2PO4, Na2HPO4, and H3BO3 were obtained from Aladin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). All chemicals were used without any further purification. The ultrapure water used throughout all the experiments was purified through Water Purifier Nanopure water system (18.3MΩ.cm).

The morphology and size of −OOC-C-dots were analyzed using the transmission electron microscopy (TEM, Hitachi H-7000, operated at 80kV). Fluorescent spectra were performed by a fluorescence spectrometer F-2500 (Hitachi, Japan). The X-ray diffraction (XRD) measurements of the carbon dots were recovered using a D8 ADVANCE X-ray diffractometer (Bruker AXS, German) with Cu-Kα radiation (40kV, 40mA, λ=1.5418Å) at a scanning rate of 1° min−1 in the range from 5° to 80°. The Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR) spectrum of the −OOC-C-dots was recorded using a FT-IR200 spectrometer (Thermo, America) with KBr pellets technique, over the range 500–4000cm−1. The 13C NMR spectrum was recorded using a Avance III HD 500MHz Superconducting FT NMR Spectrometer with the freeze-dried −OOC-C-dots samples dissolved in 99.96% D2O in a 5-mm tube. The quantum yields (QYs) of the prepared carbon dots were determined using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (literature QY: 54.6%) as the standard sample by comparing the integrated fluorescence intensities (excitation at 360nm) and absorbance values at 360nm of the carbon dots aqueous solutions with those of quinine sulfate [38].

The −OOC-C-dots were synthesized according to a literature procedure [39,40]. Citric acid (100g) was thermolyzed under air at 180°C for 40h in a furnace. The HOOC-C-dots were obtained as an orange-brown liquid with high viscosity. The HOOC-C-dots were neutralized to pH 7 by aqueous NaOH solution (5M), resulting in an orange-brown solution of the −OOC-C-dots. No further purification was required because of the sufficient time allowing a thorough thermolysis reaction. The −OOC-C-dots were collected as yellow-orange powder by freeze-drying with a high yield.

Typically, the assay solutions contained 0.2mg/ml −OOC-C-dots and various concentrations of metal ions (Fe3+, Pb2+ or Hg2+) in the corresponding buffer solutions (Fe3+ in HAc-NaAc, Pb2+ in Britton-Robinson and Hg2+ in PBS buffer solution). The fluorescence spectra of the assay solutions were collected at room temperature. The signal output value was calculated according to the relative fluorescence intensity (F0-F)/F0, in which F0 and F represented the fluorescence intensities of −OOC-C-dots system at 460nm in the absence and presence of the corresponding metal ions, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of multi-metal ions in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate the feasibility of the −OOC-C-dot-based sensor for the detection of multi metal ions in an artificial system, mineral water and tap water samples were analyzed using the present method. The mineral water samples drawn from crevice in the granite of Mt. Laoshan were obtained from Qingdao Laoshan Mineral Water Co., Ltd. The tap water samples were obtained from our lab. All water samples without any pretreatment were spiked with the different concentrations of the corresponding metal ions.

In the present work, the water soluble carbon dots with a high yield were synthesized by a simple method that citric acid was thermolyzed under air at 180°C for 40h. The reaction time was sufficient to allow the reaction to thoroughly complete. Citric acid as a precursor is economic and easily available, and beneficial to provide carboxyl groups onto the surface of the carbon dots. TEM image showed that the as-synthesized −OOC-C-dots were uniform black dots and well separated from one another with near-spherical morphology (see Fig. 1 ). The diameter of the −OOC-C-dots ranged from 3.5 to 14.3nm by counting about 80 particles. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern of the −OOC-C-dots was collected to identify their crystalline nature, as shown in Fig. 2 A. A broad band centered at 25.8° (d=0.34nm) was observed, which is consistent with the (002) lattice spacing of graphitic-like carbon based materials [41]. FTIR spectra were performed to analyze the surface functional groups of the as-synthesized carbon dots (see Fig. 2B). Two characteristic absorption bands centered at 3320 and 1020cm−1 were attributed to −OH and CO stretching vibrations. The characteristic absorption peak of CH stretching vibration was observed at 2952cm−1 [42]. Two strong peaks at 1394 and 1563cm−1 belonged to the symmetric and asymmetric stretching vibrations of the carboxylate groups [43]. The peaks at 170−190ppm in the 13C NMR spectrum indicate the substantial presence of the carboxylate groups (Fig. S1). Therefore, that the surface of the as-synthesized carbon dots was terminated with carboxylate groups could be confirmed by the FTIR and 13C NMR results.

The emission of the −OOC-C-dots showed an excitation-dependent behavior, as demonstrated in Fig. 3 . With an increase in the excitation wavelength from 300 to 500nm, the emission peaks were red-shifted from 420 (blue) to 550nm (green) while their intensities decreased remarkably. The optimal emission peaked at 460nm under the excitation at 360nm. Quantum yield of the −OOC-C-dots under excitation at 360nm was measured to be 2.6% using quinine sulfate in 0.1M H2SO4 (literature QY: 54.6%) as reference.

The effect of pH on the fluorescence emission of the −OOC-C-dots was investigated by measuring the intensities of the solutions at different pH values. As shown in Fig. 4 A, the fluorescence intensity of the −OOC-C-dots increased with an increase in pH, ranging from 3.0 to 5.0. However, the fluorescence intensity of the −OOC-C-dots showed pH independence in a wide pH range from 5.0 to 12.0. Therefore, the detection of metal ions in the above range would not suffer from pH variation. The effect of ionic strength on the fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots was also investigated (see Fig. 4B). No significant change was found in the fluorescence intensity of the −OOC-C-dots with the NaCl concentration increasing from 0 to 400mM, suggesting that ionic strength will have negligible effect on the detection of metal ions at various concentrations.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The −OOC-C-dots based fluorescent chemosensor for the detection of multi-metal ions
ce:section.ce:para: In order to evaluate its potential in the detection of metal ions, the selectivity of the −OOC-C-dots based fluorescent chemosensor was investigated. Firstly, the effects of 19 kinds of cations, including Ag+, Al3+, Ba2+, Hg2+, K+, Na+, Li+, Yb3+, NH4 +, Zn2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Co2+, Cr3+, Fe3+, and Mn2+, on the fluorescence response of the −OOC-C-dots in HNO3 solution at pH=5.0 were explored. As shown in Fig. 5 A, Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, and Fe3+ ions significantly quenched the fluorescence emission of the carbon dots. As a result, it is impossible to selectively detect any metal ion in HNO3 solution at pH=5.0 because interferences will come from other metal ions. However, in the NaAc-HAc buffer solution at pH=5.0, most cations exerted no effects on the fluorescence emission, except that Fe3+ significantly quenched the fluorescence of the −OOC-C-dots (see Fig. 5B). Meanwhile, the fluorescence of the −OOC-C-dots was quenched by Pb2+, while other metal ions showed negligible effects on the fluorescence emission of the carbon dots in Britton-Robinson buffer solution at pH=5.0 (see Fig. 5C). The fluorescence of the carbon dots showed selective response to Hg2+ in PBS buffer solution at pH=5.0 (Fig. 5D). Besides, the NaAc-HAc, Britton-Robinson, and PBS buffer solutions themselves exerted no effects on the fluorescence of the −OOC-C-dots (Fig. S2). Therefore, the selectivity of the −OOC-C-dots based fluorescent chemosensor can be switched to Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer solution, to Pb2+ in Britton-Robinson buffer solution, and to Hg2+ in PBS solution.

The reason that Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, and Fe3+ ions caused fluorescence quenching of the −OOC-C-dots in HNO3 solution at pH=5.0 can be attributed to the nonspecific interactions between the carboxylate groups and the metal ions (forming non-fluorescent complexes with the −OOC-C-dots) [44]. It is confirmed by the fact that the fluorescence intensity can be recovered when EDTA, a competitive chelator, is added to the −OOC-C-dots solutions quenched by the metal ions (See Fig. S3 and S4) [45]. Due to this coordination interaction, electrons in the excited state of the −OOC-C-dots would transfer to the unfilled orbitals of the metal ions and lead to nonradiative electron/hole recombination, which results in fluorescence quenching [46].

Moreover, high selectivity of the −OOC-C-dots to Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer solution, to Pb2+ in Britton-Robinson buffer solution, and to Hg2+ in PBS solution is ascribed to masking effects of the buffer solutions. As a result, Fe3+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ have stronger binding affinity and faster chelating kinetics with carboxylic groups on the surfaces of the −OOC-C-dots, compared with the other metal ions in the respective buffer solutions [46]. The selective binding affinities between the −OOC-C-dots and the metal ions (Fe3+, Pb2+ and Hg2+) based on masking effects of the buffer solutions are supported by the experimental results that EDTA can recover the fluorescence of the −OOC-C-dots quenched by Fe3+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ in the corresponding buffer solution by competing for the metal ions to form more stable complexes (Fig. S4) [45,46].

The effect of reaction time on the fluorescence intensity was also investigated by continuously measuring the intensities after Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ ions were added into their corresponding buffer solutions. 20min later, no obvious change in intensity was observed (data not shown). Accordingly, the optimum reaction time was selected to be 20min.

Based on the switchable selectivity of the −OOC-C-dots, different concentrations of metal ions (Fe3+, Pb2+, Hg2+) were added to NaAc-HAc, Britton-Robinson, and PBS buffer solutions of the −OOC-C-dots at pH=5.0, respectively, and the fluorescence emission intensities were measured to study the sensitivities of the −OOC-C-dots based fluorescent chemosensor to Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in the corresponding buffer solutions. As shown in Fig. 6 A, C and E, the emission fluorescence of the −OOC-C-dots showed a decrease in intensity upon the addition of different concentrations of Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer solution, Pb2+ in Britton-Robinson buffer solution, and Hg2+ in PBS buffer solution ranging from 0 to 800μM, respectively. Fig. 6B, D and F present (F0-F)/F0 versus the concentrations of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in NaAc-HAc, Britton-Robinson, and PBS buffer solutions, respectively. A good linear correlation (R2 =0.996) was obtained in the concentration range from 0.4 to 100μM for Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer solution. The linear regression equation was (F0-F)/F0 =0.0137+0.0025c (c represents the concentration of Fe3+), and the limit of detection (LOD) was estimated to be 2.8μM based on three times the standard deviation rule (LOD=3Sd/s). For Pb2+ in Britton-Robinson solution, there was a good linear relationship between (F0-F)/F0 and the concentration in the range from 100 to 600μM with a linear regression equation (F0-F)/F0 =0.0632+0.00069c (R2 =0.998), and the LOD was estimated to be 7.2μM. A linear curve of (F0-F)/F0 and concentration, ((F0-F)/F0 =0.0023c − 0.0211, R2 =0.999), was calibrated in the range from 20 to 200μM for Hg2+ in PBS solution and the LOD was calculated to be 5.7μM. Thus, the −OOC-C-dots can potentially be developed to be a chemosensor for the selective detection of Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer solution, Pb2+ in Britton-Robinson buffer solution, and Hg2+ in PBS solution by using a turn-off mode.

Due to the selectivity of the −OOC-C-dots based fluorescent chemosensor can be switched to Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer solution, to Pb2+ in Britton-Robinson buffer solution, and to Hg2+ in PBS buffer solution combined with high sensitivity, the present chemosensor might be directly applied for detecting Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in real water samples. To evaluate the feasibility of the −OOC-C-dots-based sensor for the detection of multi metal ions in an artificial system, the mineral water samples drawn from crevice in the granite of Mt. Laoshan (obtained from Qingdao Laoshan Mineral Water Co. Ltd) and tap water samples from our lab were analyzed by the present method. The mineral water and the tap water samples without any pretreatment were spiked with Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ at different concentrations, respectively. As presented in Table 1 , the recoveries for the individual detection of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in the spiked water samples ranged from 96.6% to 108.7%. In order to further assess the possibility of the −OOC-C-dots-based sensor for the selective detection of Fe3+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ in the presence of various interfering ions, the ions including Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, and Fe3+ which have quenching effect on the sensor were added into the mineral water and the tap water samples. The total concentration of the interfering ions is 210μM in the samples for the detection of Fe3+ and 350μM for the detection of Pb2+ and Hg2+. Then the selective detection of Fe3+, Pb2+ and Hg2+ in the samples was individually performed using −OOC-C-dots-based sensor in NaAc-HAc buffer solution, Britton-Robinson buffer solution and PBS buffer solution. As demonstrated in Table 2 , the recoveries for the selective detection of multi metal ions in the presence of various interfering ions were in the range of 95.9% to 106.3%. By comparison with the results in Table 1, the −OOC-C-dot-based sensor can be developed as a promising platform for the selective detection of multi-metal ions in real samples, even in the presence of high concentration of interfering ions.

The surface of the carbon dots synthesized by hydrothermal pyrolysis of citric acid at 180°C for 40h under air is terminated with carboxylate groups. Although the blue fluorescence emission of the −OOC-C-dots is quenched by Al3+, Hg2+, Pb2+, Ni2+, Cu2+, Cr3+, Co2+, and Fe3+ ions in HNO3 solution at pH=5.0, the selectivity of the −OOC-C-dots based chemosensor can be switched to Fe3+ in NaAc-HAc buffer solution, to Pb2+ in Britton-Robinson buffer solution, and to Hg2+ in PBS solution. Moreover, good linear relationships are found between (F0-F)/F0 and the concentrations of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in NaAc-HAc, Britton-Robinson, and PBS buffer solutions, respectively. And the limits of the detection for Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in the corresponding buffer systems are as low as 2.8μM, 7.2μM, and 5.7μM. By the proposed method, the selective detection of Fe3+, Pb2+, and Hg2+ in the mineral water and tap water samples, even in the presence of different types of interfering ions at high concentration, can be accurately achieved. Thus, a new method for the detection of multi-metal ions in real samples using one carbon-dot-based chemosensor may be potentially developed with switchable selectivity in different buffer solutions.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 31572181), Project for Science and Technology Development of Shandong Province (No. 2014GSF121034), Natural Science Foundation of Shandong Province (No. ZR2014CM012), Opening Fund of State Key Laboratory of Rare Earth Utilization (RERU2013005), and The Project-sponsored by SRF for ROCS, SEM.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.snb.2016.09.068.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: The following is Supplementary data to this article:

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Malabsorption and defective utilization of iron in three siblings
Association of HLA-A3 and HLA-B14 antigens with idiopathic haemochromatosis
Iron metabolism: iron deficiency and iron overload
A fluorescent biosensor based on carbon dots-labeled oligodeoxyribonucleotide and graphene oxide for mercury (II) detection
N-doped carbon dots synthesized by rapid microwave irradiation as highly fluorescent probes for Pb2+detection
A turn-on coumarin-based fluorescent sensor with high selectivity for mercury ions in aqueous media
Synthesis and application of imprinted polyvinylimidazole-silica hybrid copolymer for Pb2+ determination by flow-injection thermospray flame furnace atomic absorption spectrometry

New method for mercury determination in microwave digested soil samples based on cold vapor capacitively coupled plasma microtorch optical emission spectrometry: comparison with atomic fluorescence spectrometry
Speciation of mercury in water samples by dispersive liquid–liquid microextraction combined with high performance liquid chromatography-inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry

Hydrothermal treatment of grass: a low-cost, green route to nitrogen-doped, carbon-rich, photoluminescent polymer nanodots as an effective fluorescent sensing platform for label-free detection of Cu(II) ions
Highly photoluminescent carbon dots-based fluorescent chemosensors for sensitive and selective detection of mercury ions and application of imaging in living cells
A highly selective and simple fluorescent sensor for mercury (II) ion detection based on cysteamine-capped CdTe quantum dots synthesized by the reflux method
Europium-decorated graphene quantum dots as a fluorescent probe for label-free, rapid and sensitive detection of Cu2+ and L-cysteine
Carbon quantum dots-based recyclable real-time fluorescence assay for alkaline phosphatase with adenosine triphosphate as substrate
Ultra-small fluorescent metal nanoclusters: synthesis and biological applications
Time-dependent intracellular trafficking of FITC-conjugated epigallocatechin-3-O-gallate in L-929 cells
Immunofluorescent labeling of cancer marker Her2 and other cellular targets with semiconductor quantum dots
Heterogeneous nano metal-organic framework fluorescence probe for highly selective and sensitive detection of hydrogen sulfide in living cells
Crosslinked carbon dots as ultra-bright fluorescence probes
Facile preparation of low cytotoxicity fluorescent carbon nanocrystals by electrooxidation of graphite
Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence
Synthesis of biocompatible and highly photoluminescent nitrogen doped carbon dots from lime: analytical applications and optimization using response surface methodology
Common origin of green luminescence in carbon nanodots and graphene quantum dots
Fluorescent carbon nanoparticles: synthesis, characterization, and bioimaging application
Economical, green synthesis of fluorescent carbon nanoparticles and their use as probes for sensitive and selective detection of mercury(II) ions
Germanium-doped carbon dots as a new type of fluorescent probe for visualizing the dynamic invasions of mercury(II) ions into cancer cells
Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions
Chemically tailoring coal to fluorescent carbon dots with tuned size and their capacity for Cu(II) detection
Solid-phase synthesis of highly fluorescent nitrogen-doped carbon dots for sensitive and selective probing ferric ions in living cells
Comparative study for N and S doped carbon dots: synthesis, characterization and applications for Fe3+ probe and cellular imaging
Synthesis of fluorescent carbon dots via simple acid hydrolysis of bovine serum albumin and its potential as sensitive sensing probe for lead (II) ions
On-off-on fluorescent carbon dot nanosensor for recognition of chromium(VI) and ascorbic acid based on the inner filter effect
Bright-yellow-emissive N-Doped carbon dots: preparation, cellular imaging, and bifunctional sensing
High-quality carbon dots: synthesis, peroxidase-like activity and their application in the detection of H2O2, Ag+ and Fe3+
Amino acid derived highly luminescent, heteroatom-doped carbon dots for label-free detection of Cd2+/Fe3+, cell imaging and enhanced antibacterial activity
Polymer nanodots of graphitic carbon nitride as effective fluorescent probes for the detection of Fe3+ and Cu2+ ions
Determination of the fluorescence quantum yield of quantum dots: suitable procedures and achievable uncertainties
Na+-functionalized carbon quantum dots: a new draw solute in forward osmosis for seawater desalination
Solar hydrogen production using carbon quantum dots and a molecular nickel catalyst
Green synthesized carbon nanodots as a fluorescent probe for selective and sensitive detection of iron(III) ions
FTIR, FT-Raman spectral analysis and normal coordinate calculations of 2-hydroxy-3-methoxybenzaldehyde thiosemicarbozone
Ionothermal synthesis of crystalline, condensed, graphitic carbon nitride
A fluorescent turn-off/on method based on carbon dots as fluorescent probes for the sensitive determination of Pb2+ and pyrophosphate in an aqueous solution
Facile synthesis of carbon nanodots from ethanol and their application in ferric(iii) ion assay
Facile synthesis of nitrogen-doped carbon dots for Fe3+ sensing and cellular imaging

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Chun Li was born in Zhaodong City, Heilongjiang Province, China. He received his BSc from Kunming University of Science and Technology in 2014. He joined Prof. Jinping Wang’s group at Qingdao Agricultural University as a graduate student in 2015. His current research focuses on synthesis of carbon dots and their applications in biosensors and bioimaging.

Weijian Liu received her BSc in Material Chemistry from Qingdao Agricultural University in 2015. She is currently a graduate student at Qingdao Agricultural University, majoring in applied chemistry. Her research interests are primarily in preparation of fluorescent probes, and their applications in analytical chemistry.

Yanjing Ren received her BSc in Bioscience from Dezhou University in 2015. She is currently a graduate student at Qingdao Agricultural University, under the supervision of Prof. Jinping Wang. Her research involves the development of carbon dots for biological imaging applications.

Xiaobo Sun received his Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2005. Now she is an Associate Professor in College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical sciences at Qingdao Agricultural University, China. Her research interests are primarily in analytical chemistry and separation science.

Wei Pan was born in Shandong, China, in 1979. He received his MSc from Sichuan University in 2006. Then he joined Qingdao Agricultural University in Sep. 2006 as an experimentalist. His research interests focus on functional nanomaterials and devices.

Jinping Wang received his Ph.D. from Changchun Institute of Applied Chemistry, Chinese Academy of Sciences, in 2004. He received postdoctoral training at Waseda University in 2007–2009, and worked as a visiting scholar at Clemson University in 2012–2013. Now he is a Full Professor in College of Chemistry and Pharmaceutical sciences at Qingdao Agricultural University, China. His research interests are primarily in preparation of carbon-based nanomaterials, and their bioimaging and sensing applications.
